Faith in Christ and love for him should unite believers more closely than any external relationship can unite people of the world. Paul remembered his friends in his private prayers. We must remember Christian friends often, as their situations may require, keeping them in our thoughts and on our hearts before God. Differences in opinions or practices that are not essential should not affect affection for the truth. He inquired about his friends’ well-being, growth, and fruitfulness of their graces, their faith in Christ, and love for him and all believers. The good which Philemon did brought joy and comfort to him and others, who therefore desired that he would continue and increase in good works, to God's honor.

It does not demean anyone to humble yourself, and sometimes even to beg, where you might rightfully command; the apostle argues from love rather than authority on behalf of one converted through his means: this was Onesimus. Referring to that name, which signifies “profitable,” the apostle acknowledges that in the past he had been unprofitable to Philemon, but quickly mentions the change by which he had become profitable. Unrighteous people are unprofitable; they do not fulfill their purpose. But what wonderful changes conversion makes! From evil, good; from unprofitable, useful. Religious servants are treasures in a family. They will be conscientious about their time and responsibilities, and manage everything for the best. No expectation of usefulness should lead anyone to neglect their obligations or fail in obedience to superiors. One significant evidence of true repentance is returning to practice duties that have been neglected. When Onesimus was unrepentant, he had withdrawn to his master’s detriment; but now that he had seen his sin and repented, he was willing and eager to return to his duty. People often do not know the purposes for which the Lord allows some to change their circumstances or undertake ventures, perhaps from selfish motives. Had not the Lord intervened in some of our ungodly plans, we could reflect on situations where our destruction would have been certain.

When discussing the nature of any sin or offense against God, do not diminish its evil; but as God covers it in a repentant sinner, so must we. Such transformed individuals often become a blessing to all around them. Christianity does not eliminate our duties to others, but directs us to fulfill them properly. True penitents will openly acknowledge their faults, as Onesimus undoubtedly did to Paul when he was awakened and brought to repentance, especially in cases of harm done to others. The fellowship of believers does not negate the distinction of property. This passage illustrates one person bearing what another has incurred; and one becoming responsible for another through a voluntary commitment, so that he might be freed from the punishment due to his crimes, according to the doctrine that Christ willingly bore the penalty for our sins, so that we might receive the reward of his righteousness. Philemon was Paul's son in the faith, yet he entreated him as a brother. Onesimus was a poor slave, yet Paul pleaded for him as if seeking something great for himself. Christians should do what brings joy to one another’s hearts. They expect trouble from the world; they should find comfort and joy in each other. When our blessings are taken away, our trust and hope must be in God. We must diligently use available resources, and if none are present, abound in prayer. Yet, even though prayer is effective, it does not earn what is obtained. And if Christians do not meet on earth, the grace of the Lord Jesus will still be with their spirits, and they will soon meet before the throne to join forever in admiring the riches of redeeming love. The example of Onesimus may encourage the most wicked sinners to return to God, but it is a shameful encouragement if anyone persists in evil because of it. Are not many taken away in their sins, while others become more hardened? Do not resist present convictions, lest they never return.

Believers have never found greater enjoyment of God than when suffering together for him. Grace is the best wish for ourselves and others; with this the apostle begins and ends. All grace comes from Christ; he purchased it and he gives it. What more do we need to be happy than to have the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ within us? Let us do now what we should do at our last breath. Then people are ready to renounce the world, and to prefer even a small portion of grace and faith before a kingdom.
